extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **serial robot MABI MAX 100** with **six axes**, designed for **machining operations** in industrial automation. Its operational domain revolves around **cyber-physical system (CPS) integration**, where it interacts with physical components to execute precise motion control tasks. Key characteristics include its modular architecture, support for **friction modeling** (specifically for the first axis), and alignment with **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies for structured development. The robot’s physical twin is augmented with a **digital shadow** that passively aggregates contextual data traces (e.g., sensor inputs) for analysis without autonomous intervention or bidirectional communication with the physical system. The system’s complexity is managed through **multi-level abstraction** (requirements, functional/logical architecture, product/technical layers) using **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **MontiArc** and **SysML** for component and connector modeling, complemented by **executable simulation models** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) for behavior validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin (DT) system provides **passive, one-way data integration** services for the physical robot MABI MAX 100, adhering strictly to the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** as defined in Kritzinger et al. [32]. Key services include:
  1. **Live Data Aggregation**: Continuous collection and aggregation of contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs) from the robot’s axes, enabling real-time monitoring of system state.
  2. **Friction Identification**: Computational processing of input traces to derive friction characteristics (e.g., for the first axis) via **executable simulation models** (MathWorks Simulink) or data calculation models, ensuring traceability to processing components.
  3. **Visualization**: Generation of abstracted data representations (e.g., UML class diagrams, performance metrics) for stakeholders to interpret system behavior without autonomous control or feedback loops. The DT does not support **closed-loop control**, **optimization algorithms**, or **predictive analytics**—these are excluded due to its passive nature.

**Computational Models and Data Components:**
  A Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **engineering models** (geometric, behavioral, analytical) and **simulation/data calculation models** to represent the physical system’s structure, properties, and behaviors. These models are categorized into:
  - **Engineering Models**: Describe the system’s architecture via **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc) at abstraction levels like **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. ADLs (Architecture Description Languages) define components, interfaces, connectors, and behavior (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automata).
  - **Simulation Models**: Used for physics-based (white-box) simulations (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools like [29], [30]) to model nonlinear dynamics (e.g., robot friction in industrial processes). These models ensure interpretability and predictability but may lack granularity for complex phenomena.
  - **Data-Driven Models**: Complement white-box models via **machine learning (ML)** or probabilistic latent variable models (e.g., adaptive control frameworks) to capture non-linear relationships (e.g., robot friction) that analytical models cannot represent. Data traces are aggregated and abstracted via **purpose-driven digital shadows**, where metadata supports traceability (e.g., linking data traces to processing components via **originates-from associations**).

  **Data Management**:
  Data traces are processed through **Processing components** (e.g., data calculation or simulation models) to generate output traces. Traceability is maintained via **transitive associations**, ensuring a data trace’s origin can be traced back to its generating component, consumed inputs, and involved models. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) formalizes this structure, allowing customization via **concretization** (e.g., mapping reference concepts to concrete elements in Class Diagrams).

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
